questions.text: The production of new individuals from parents is known as:
questions.explanation: Reproduction ensures the continuity of a species.

questions.text: Reproduction without the production of seeds is called:
questions.explanation: In asexual reproduction, only one parent is involved.

questions.text: Reproduction through seeds is called:
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction involve the fusion of male and female gametes.

questions.text: Which of the following is a type of asexual reproduction where plants are produced from roots, stems, or leaves?
questions.explanation: Vegetative parts like potato eyes or ginger rhizomes are used.

questions.text: In yeast, reproduction occurs by:
questions.explanation: A small bulb-like projection called a bud grows and detaches from the parent cell.

questions.text: Algae like Spirogyra reproduce by:
questions.explanation: The organism breaks into two or more fragments, which grow into new individuals.

questions.text: Fungi on bread reproduce through:
questions.explanation: Spores are asexual reproductive bodies protected by a hard coat.

questions.text: The reproductive part of a plant is the:
questions.explanation: Flowers contain the male and female reproductive organs.

questions.text: The male reproductive part of a flower is the:
questions.explanation: Stamen consists of anther and filament.

questions.text: The female reproductive part of a flower is the:
questions.explanation: Pistil (or carpel) consists of stigma, style, and ovary.

questions.text: Flowers that contain either only stamens or only pistil are called:
questions.explanation: Examples include corn, papaya, and cucumber.

questions.text: Flowers that contain both stamens and pistil are called:
questions.explanation: Examples include mustard, rose, and petunia.

questions.text: Anther contains ______ which produce male gametes.
questions.explanation: Pollen grains are carried to the stigma for pollination.

questions.text: Ovary contains one or more ______.
questions.explanation: The female gamete or egg is formed in an ovule.

questions.text: The transfer of pollen grains from anther to stigma is called:
questions.explanation: Pollination is the first step toward sexual reproduction in plants.

questions.text: If pollen lands on the stigma of the same flower, it is called:
questions.explanation: Self-pollination occurs within the same flower or another flower on the same plant.

questions.text: If pollen lands on the stigma of a flower of a different plant of the same kind, it is:
questions.explanation: Cross-pollination promotes genetic diversity.

questions.text: The fusion of male and female gametes to form a zygote is called:
questions.explanation: This occurs inside the ovule after the pollen tube reaches it.

questions.text: After fertilization, the ovary grows into a:
questions.explanation: The fruit is the ripened ovary protecting the seeds.

questions.text: After fertilization, the ovules develop into:
questions.explanation: Seeds contain the embryo and stored food.

questions.text: Which of the following is a method of seed dispersal?
questions.explanation: Dispersal prevents competition between the parent and offspring.

questions.text: Winged seeds like those of maple are dispersed by:
questions.explanation: Wings help the seeds glide and travel long distances in the air.

questions.text: Spiny seeds with hooks (like Xanthium) are dispersed by:
questions.explanation: Hooks attach to the fur of animals or clothing of humans.

questions.text: Seeds of coconut are dispersed by:
questions.explanation: Coconuts have a fibrous outer coat that allows them to float on water.

questions.text: Which plant reproduces through leaves?
questions.explanation: Bryophyllum has buds in the margins of its leaves.

questions.text: Potato reproduces by its 'eyes', which are actually:
questions.explanation: These buds can grow into new potato plants.

questions.text: The 'rhizome' is a vegetative part of:
questions.explanation: Rhizomes are underground modified stems.

questions.text: Plants like roses and grapes are usually grown by:
questions.explanation: Cutting is a common method of vegetative propagation.

questions.text: Bread mould reproduces through spores. Spores are:
questions.explanation: Spores can withstand unfavorable conditions.

questions.text: The cells that result from the fusion of gametes is called:
questions.explanation: Zygote is the first cell of a new organism.

questions.text: Balsam and castor seeds are dispersed by:
questions.explanation: The fruits burst suddenly, scattering seeds away from the parent.

questions.text: A complete flower is one that has:
options: All four whorls (sepals, petals, stamens, pistil)
questions.explanation: It possesses both reproductive and non-reproductive whorls.

questions.text: The sticky part of the pistil that receives pollen is:
questions.explanation: Stigma is specialized to capture pollen grains.

questions.text: Ferns and mosses reproduce by:
questions.explanation: They are non-flowering plants that use spore formation.

questions.text: Seed dispersal helps in:
options: Prevents competition for light and water
questions.explanation: Dispersal is vital for the survival and expansion of plant species.

questions.text: Male gamete is found in ______.
questions.explanation: Pollen contains the male sperm cells.

questions.text: Female gamete is found in ______.
questions.explanation: Ovule contains the egg cell.

questions.text: The part that develops into an embryo is:
questions.explanation: The zygote divides and develops into an embryo inside the seed.

questions.text: Fleshly fruits are exemplified by:
questions.explanation: These fruits have a soft, succulent pericarp.

questions.text: Dry fruits include:
questions.explanation: These have a hard and dry pericarp at maturity.
